subject: Physics
chapterSummary: This chapter explores the nature of light and how it interacts with surfaces through reflection and refraction. We study how spherical mirrors and lenses form images, learn the essential formulas for predicting image positions, and explore real-world applications like vehicle mirrors and spectacles.

subtopics.title: What is Light?
subtopics.summary: Light is a form of energy that enables us to see objects through reflection and straight-line propagation.
subtopics.theory: Light is something we perceive every day, yet its nature is complex. It typically travels in straight lines, which we represent as 'rays'. While we often treat light as a ray, at very small scales, it can bend around objects (diffraction), which suggests a wave nature. Modern quantum theory reconciles this by explaining that light has both particle and wave properties. For this chapter, we focus on its straight-line propagation to understand reflection (bouncing off surfaces) and refraction (bending when entering a new medium).
subtopics.didYouKnow: Light is the fastest thing in the universe, travelling at nearly 3,00,000 kilometres per second! In the time it takes you to blink, light could circle the Earth 7 times.

example.title: Seeing index in a Dark Room
example.description: Have you noticed you can't see objects in a pitch-black room? You only see them when a bulb is switched on because light reflects off the 'chai' cup or 'auto-rickshaw' toy and enters your eyes.

keyTerms.definition: The straight-line path along which light travels.
The bouncing back of light when it strikes a highly polished surface like a mirror.

quickCheck.question: What happens when light strikes a highly polished surface?
quickCheck.answer: It undergoes reflection, meaning it bounces back into the same medium.

subtopics.title: Spherical Mirrors: Concave and Convex
subtopics.summary: Spherical mirrors are curved reflecting surfaces classified into concave (curved inwards) and convex (curved outwards).
subtopics.theory: Unlike flat plane mirrors, spherical mirrors are curved. A mirror whose reflecting surface is curved inwards (like the inside of a spoon) is a 'Concave Mirror'. A mirror curved outwards (like the back of a spoon) is a 'Convex Mirror'. Key terms include the 'Pole' (center of reflecting surface), 'Center of Curvature' (the center of the sphere the mirror belongs to), and 'Principal Focus' (the point where parallel rays meet or appear to come from after reflection).
subtopics.didYouKnow: The shiny surface of a steel spoon can act as both a concave and a convex mirror depending on which side you look at!

example.title: The Spoon Test
example.description: Look at your face in the inner side of a large 'shiny spoon'. Your image appears inverted. Now flip it to the outer side; your image becomes upright and small. This is the difference between concave and convex reflections.

keyTerms.definition: A spherical mirror with an inward-curved reflecting surface.
A spherical mirror with an outward-curved reflecting surface.

formula.plain: Radius of Curvature = 2 x Focal Length

quickCheck.question: If the focal length of a mirror is 10 cm, what is its radius of curvature?

subtopics.title: The Mirror Formula and Magnification
subtopics.summary: The mirror formula relates object distance, image distance, and focal length to predict where an image will form.
subtopics.theory: The Mirror Formula is `\frac{1}{v} + \frac{1}{u} = \frac{1}{f}`. Here, 'u' is object distance, 'v' is image distance, and 'f' is focal length. We use the 'New Cartesian Sign Convention' where distances in the direction of incident light are positive and against it are negative. Magnification (m) tells us how large the image is compared to the object, calculated as `m = \frac{h'}{h}` or `m = -\frac{v}{u}`. A negative 'm' means the image is real and inverted, while a positive 'm' means it's virtual and erect.
subtopics.didYouKnow: Mirrors in shops and malls are often convex because they give a much wider field of view, helping shopkeepers see the entire store in one glance!

example.title: Rear-View Mirrors
example.description: Rear-view mirrors in cars and bikes are convex mirrors because they always produce small, upright images, allowing the driver to see traffic coming from far away clearly.

keyTerms.definition: A set of rules for assigning signs (+ or -) to distances in optics.
The ratio of the height of the image to the height of the object.

quickCheck.question: If a mirror produces a magnification of -2, what does it tell you about the image?
quickCheck.answer: The negative sign means the image is real and inverted, and the '2' means it is twice the size of the object (magnified).

subtopics.title: Refraction: Why Light Bends
subtopics.summary: Refraction is the bending of light as it passes from one transparent medium to another due to a change in speed.
subtopics.theory: When light enters obliquely from one medium (like air) into another (like water or glass), it changes direction. This is 'Refraction'. This happens because the speed of light changes between materials. Light slows down in 'optically denser' media (like glass) and bends towards the normal. It speeds up in 'optically rarer' media (like air) and bends away from the normal. 'Snell’s Law' states that the ratio of the sine of the angle of incidence to the sine of the angle of refraction is constant, known as the 'Refractive Index'.
subtopics.didYouKnow: Bending light is the reason why a straight pencil looks broken when you put it in a glass of water, and why an 'auto-rickshaw' on a hot road might look like it's in a puddle (mirage)!

example.title: Raised Coins
example.description: Have you noticed that a coin at the bottom of a bucket of water looks slightly higher than it actually is? This is an optical illusion caused by light refracting as it leaves the water.

keyTerms.definition: The bending of a ray of light when it passes obliquely from one medium to another.
A measure of how much a medium slows down or speeds up light compared to a vacuum.

formula.plain: Refractive Index = Speed of light in vacuum / Speed of light in medium

variables.meaning: Speed of light in vacuum (3x10^8 m/s)
Speed of light in the medium

quickCheck.question: Which medium has a higher refractive index: Glass or Air?
quickCheck.answer: Glass (approx 1.50) has a much higher refractive index than Air (approx 1.0003).

subtopics.title: Spherical Lenses and Power
subtopics.summary: Lenses use refraction to converge or diverge light, used in glasses to correct vision.
subtopics.theory: A lens is a transparent material with at least one spherical surface. 'Convex Lenses' are thicker in the middle and converge rays (converging lens). 'Concave Lenses' are thinner in the middle and diverge rays (diverging lens). The 'Lens Formula' is `\frac{1}{v} - \frac{1}{u} = \frac{1}{f}`. The 'Power of a Lens' (P) is the reciprocal of its focal length in meters (`P = \frac{1}{f}`). Its unit is 'Dioptre' (D). A convex lens has positive power, while a concave lens has negative power.
subtopics.didYouKnow: The lens in your eye is actually flexible! Your eye muscles squeeze it to change its focal length so you can focus on both your 'mobile screen' and the distant sky.

example.title: Spectacles
example.description: If your friend wears glasses, they are using lenses to correct their vision. A student who can't see the blackboard clearly might be using a concave lens with 'negative power'.

keyTerms.definition: A converging lens that is thicker at the center than at the edges.
The SI unit of power of a lens.

quickCheck.question: What is the power of a lens with a focal length of 2 meters?

importantFormulas.meaning: Relationship between Radius of Curvature and Focal Length

connectsTo: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
